document.title: Librarian2 Resource Registry Schema
document.purpose: Define the schema and design principles for Librarian2 resource registries, which allow LLMs and humans to discover resources on a developer's system.
document.author: Lion + ChatGPT

overview.description: Librarian2 is a desktop-scale resource registry designed to help both humans and LLM-based tools locate useful resources on a developer's system. A registry is a JSON file that contains entries describing files, folders, programs, URLs, and other resources.
overview.primary_goal: Allow LLMs and humans to quickly locate important artifacts such as specifications, repositories, tools, and datasets.
design_philosophy: Registries primarily point to resources rather than containing them. Inline resources are supported but expected to be rare.
The system remains flat and simple rather than becoming a knowledge graph.
Most fields are optional and extensible.
Consumers interpret metadata rather than the registry imposing behavior.

summary.description: Librarian2 registries act as structured directories of resources. They help both humans and LLMs discover important artifacts such as documentation, tools, repositories, and datasets within a developer environment. The design prioritizes simplicity, extensibility, and compatibility with both human editing and automated tooling.

typical_use_cases: Helping LLM coding agents discover project documentation
Locating specifications or schemas in a large codebase
Discovering tools or scripts available in a workspace
non_goals: Not a system for executing programs

registry_file_structure.description: A registry file is a JSON document containing metadata about the document itself and a flat dictionary of resource entries.

resource_entry_schema.description: Each resource entry describes a discoverable artifact. Entries are keyed by their ID in the registry dictionary and also contain their ID within the entry itself.
notes: Resource entries may include additional fields not described in this schema.
Consumers should ignore fields they do not recognize.

id.description: Identifier for the resource. Must match the dictionary key used in the registry. Identifiers may contain letters, digits, underscore, period, and hyphen.
notes: The identifier inside the entry must match the dictionary key.
Identifiers beginning with a single quote (e.g. '0, '1) represent positional convenience entries and are not stable identifiers.
Stable anonymous identifiers may use UUID v4 strings.

purpose.description:
Explanation of what the resource is for. This field is critical for helping LLMs decide when to consult the resource.
The purpose is ideally 1-3 sentences long, because LLMs perform much better when the field isn't extremely short or extremely long.

location.description: List of objects describing ways the resource may be accessed.
notes: The structure of this field is described in the location system section.
Each object represents one possible location or access method.
Consumers interpret the keys inside location objects.
Common keys include path, url, inline, and protocol-specific descriptors.

tags.description: Optional list of keywords that may help discovery.

description.description: Optional longer explanation of the resource.

type.description: Structured description of the resource's logical and semantic type.
notes: The structure of this field is described in the type system section.
Tools should tolerate missing or partially specified types.

invoke.description: Reserved for future execution metadata describing how to run the resource.
notes: This field is intentionally unspecified in the initial schema.
Future versions may define structured invocation instructions.

positional_ids.meaning: Convenience entries with no stable identity.
rules: Should not be referenced by other entries

type_system.description: The type field describes both the physical nature of a resource and the semantic role it plays. The field is optional, but when present it helps tools and LLMs interpret the resource more effectively.

notes: Both logical and semantic components are optional.
Tools should gracefully degrade in the presence of missing or partial type information.

logical.description: The logical type describes what the resource physically is or how it is accessed.
notes: The base field identifies the fundamental category of the resource.
Additional fields refine the logical type but are optional.
Consumers should ignore unknown fields.
Consumers should primarily rely on logical.base when determining how to handle a resource.

semantic.description: The semantic type describes the role or meaning of the resource within a project or ecosystem.
notes: Semantic types are intentionally open-ended.
Projects may introduce their own semantic vocabulary.
Consumers should ignore semantic values they do not recognize.

example_combined_type.description: Example resource type using both logical and semantic components.

location_system.description: The location field describes ways a resource may be accessed or obtained. A resource may have zero, one, or multiple locations. Each location is represented as an object inside a list. Each object describes one access method or source for the resource.
principles: A resource may have multiple locations.
Each location object represents one possible access method.
Consumers interpret location descriptors according to their own capabilities.
Unknown location keys should be ignored rather than treated as errors.

notes: Paths may be absolute or relative.
Relative paths are interpreted relative to the registry file containing the entry.

url.description: HTTP or HTTPS URL pointing to the resource.

inline.description: Inline content embedded directly within the registry.
notes: Inline resources should generally be small text artifacts.
Logical type metadata should still describe the nature of the resource (for example file/txt).
Consumers may treat inline resources as read-only content.

location.inline: You are an expert software engineer...

consumer_behavior: Consumers should attempt to interpret location descriptors they recognize.
If multiple locations are provided, consumers may choose whichever is most appropriate.
Consumers may ignore locations they do not understand.
The registry itself does not impose resolution rules.
notes: The location system intentionally avoids defining a fixed set of protocols or descriptors.
This allows projects to introduce custom descriptors without modifying the registry schema.
Because location descriptors are independent objects, additional metadata may be attached to individual locations if desired.
A resource may have zero locations if the registry entry exists only as documentation or a placeholder.

principles: New semantic types may be introduced freely.
New location descriptor keys may be introduced.
Tools should ignore unknown fields rather than failing.
Registries should remain usable even when partial.

design_principles: Keep the registry flat and simple.
Allow tools to interpret metadata flexibly.
